12187c2b6647:
Reading the opponent's civilisation from its tells: sighting a unique unit or a
starred effect identifies it, from hard upwards. The very top difficulty is
handicapped here, and says so: once the opponent has committed, sighting any of
their units or buildings reveals the civilisation -- army composition and
positions still have to be scouted.
Once identified it is never forgotten: a commitment is durable intelligence.
c8b1949c59ba:
Tells and counters are derived from the unit and civilisation tables, so adding
either is adding a row.
e2a715faea8a:
Formation placement: forward is the line from the group's centroid to the target,
and ranks are ordered by weapon reach -- melee at the front as a shield wall,
ranged progressively behind, medics last. Units with the same reach share a rank;
a loose formation widens both spacing and depth.
Only the destinations are computed here. Each unit still walks there under its
own pathing, so the formation assembles as it moves.
c1aae63849db:
Market rates drift with trading (selling pushes the price down, buying up) and
slowly return to par. Rates are per side, so each pays for its own trading.
db586d823d55:
The explored gate for placement, per side and read through the vision gate; under
omniscience everything counts as explored, symmetrically for both sides.
The previous form was written for a specific side and, with honest vision on,
left the other side unable to place a single building anywhere. It went unnoticed
only because omniscience happened to be the default at the time.
584261901c15:
Wall runs follow a Manhattan path, so there are no diagonal gaps. Each tile is
paid for separately, unbuildable tiles are skipped, and the placement mode stays
active so several runs can be laid in a row.
b3343d23f54c:
============ AI command whitelist ============
The rule-based AI may only act on the world through this set -- the player's own
commands, parameterised by side. It never touches unit internals, never teleports
anything, never conjures resources. Placement, affordability, unlock, age and
starvation checks apply to it exactly as to the player, and a handicapped tier
only changes disclosed numbers rather than bypassing a check.
c6467145e222: Building hit test by footprint rectangle, so a 2x1 gate resolves correctly.
789b0ef22a92:
Demolition refund: full while still under construction (same as cancelling), and
half, scaled by remaining health, once finished -- so a clean demolition recovers
something and a damaged building recovers less.
6d59cd30f74a:
Demolishing or cancelling a building. The last command centre cannot be
demolished, so nobody loses by accident. It clears the footprint, ejects the
garrison and releases any villagers assigned to build or repair it -- otherwise
they chase a site that no longer exists.
e879e5549740:
Ships repair slowly near a friendly dock, for free, and more slowly than a
monastery heals. It replaces having villagers repair ships, which they cannot
reach over water, and makes sailing a damaged ship home a real decision. Nothing
repairs while in contact with the enemy.
ad644688bf0f:
2.5D cliff faces. A purely 2D cliff is a zero-width line, which is both where
corner-cutting comes from and why it looks cheap. Here highland gets a real rock
face drawn along every edge against non-highland (the southern edge, facing the
camera, most prominently), with a lit lip and a cast shadow.
ce60bda578b1:
Viewport culling. Entities off screen are clipped by the canvas anyway, but each
still costs a round of fill, stroke and text calls -- a fixed waste that grows
with the army. The margin is generous enough that health bars, labels and group
rings are never clipped at the edge.
52c335623e2f:
Only our own drag paths are drawn. Nudges are set not just by the player's
gesture but by the automatic unstick logic, for both sides -- so an enemy unit
freeing itself would draw a dotted line and a destination ring inside the fog,
giving away both its position and where it was going. Spectator mode is omniscient
by definition, so it may draw them.
291c4758103f:
Fog is drawn for visible tiles only. Sweeping the whole map every frame cost
nearly ten thousand fill calls per frame regardless of what was on screen -- and
twice that while spectating, which is why long spectated games grew steadily
choppier.
e4a579017776: Dragging a group tag issues an order; dropping it on a building garrisons.
6f2b0641ec09:
Panel opening and periodic refresh (a group panel with a slider being dragged is
not refreshed, so the interaction is not interrupted).
c2decd57fc87:
Periodic redraws pause while a finger or mouse button is down: otherwise a
rebuild swaps the button out mid-click, and the first tap on a panel appears to
do nothing.
8f2405ed8480:
Production queue: each entry shows the unit and its group colour, the first is
filled to show progress, and tapping cancels it with a refund.
265c391d933e:
Production panel, shared by barracks, stable and workshop: the unit table itself
says which building trains what, so the panel filters on that.
f01c5d853b8c:
Command centre queue for villagers and scouts, behaving exactly like the barracks
queue and read per building.
f85467dfbeb2:
Command centre panel, shared by the capital, outposts and cities: villagers
everywhere, scouts where production is full, garrisoning, and upgrading an outpost
into a city.
1a079c3e7074:
The button badge counts this centre's own queue; the population cap and the HUD
use the global count.
2a0993ca4b2e:
Monastery panel: trains medics and explains the healing aura, whose radius is
shown only while the building is selected.
0874280b68b0:
====== Unit inspector (read-only): tapping one of your units shows its live
numbers with research bonuses called out. It issues no orders, keeping to
"decisions to the player, execution to the rules". ======
3e2803b9ecca:
A unique unit is a civilisation tell. What the player reads off the enemy panel
is exactly what the AI reads off theirs -- the information is symmetric.
cb7ce4b863d3:
Spectator mode is an omniscient view and discloses everything: group names,
missions, scout modes. The normal enemy panel shows none of it, because that is
what scouting is for.
0b74738becb6:
Range token: ranged units show the projectile range, medics their healing
distance, and melee units just say "melee" -- their engagement distance is not a
projectile range and printing a number invites the wrong comparison.
465f2ce8b210:
The tech tree is a read-only full-page reference. The civilisation being viewed
is for browsing only and does not affect play.
469b7b376631:
Action row: your own civilisation offers age advancement and the research queue;
viewing another offers switching to it.
aa655c6d4057:
Villagers are produced solely from the command centre queues, so the economy
panel has no production button -- one place to look, and no unresponsive-feeling
second entry point sharing the same queue.
6b27ce1547f2:
Villager behaviour, two tracks: the bell for peacetime, and under attack a repair
toggle, a garrison toggle and a three-way stance. The under-attack row reads left
to right in priority order, matching the heading.
34ce1083b775:
== Development tooling UI: coordinate readout, movement log, and the debug
toggle ==
ddad8a8e8af3:
One tools button in the bottom-left corner, away from the information panels on
the right so it never covers them.
041b1bf43b84:
== Arena: test battles on flat ground with no economy and no interference.
Pick a matchup, the two armies charge, and rerunning the same one repeatedly is
how the probabilistic parts (hit chance and the rest) get measured. ==
db1e80147a41:
Shoreline arena: land units on one side, ships on the other, with the map split
down the middle into land and water so the two shoot at each other across the
shore. It is the only way to measure land ranged units against warships at equal
cost -- a purely land or purely naval arena cannot cross domains.
a9751d8df3cd:
In a shoreline arena neither side can chase the other, so both armies have to
start inside each other's range. Spawned any further apart, they simply freeze at
the edge of their search radius and every match is a draw -- which is exactly what
happened the first time.
e612d586aad9:
Difficulty disclosure text, shared by the settings panel and the title screen so
the two cannot describe the same thing differently.
The list is generated from the difficulty table rather than written by hand:
hand-written, it silently omitted every handicap added afterwards.
01599abcff18:
Whether the opponent's civilisation can be chosen: yes below hard, random above.
Scouting is a designed decision -- identifying a committed civilisation on sight
is listed as a handicap for the very top difficulty. Letting the player pick it
from a menu would hand them that same intelligence for free and make scouting the
opponent's buildings pointless. The lower difficulties are practice, where
choosing a matchup is the point.
c481771c81ae: Fast-forward: enter a time, jump to it, pause automatically. Forward only.
a199d65ef0bc:
URL knobs for difficulty and the AI's civilisation (the real UI is the opponent
panel; what each handicapped tier gets is spelled out there).
b7dd321e39bf:
============ 16. Save snapshots (state, not replay) ============
Why not seed plus replay: a replay file is invalidated wholesale by every balance
change, and what a player wants saved is the position in front of them.
So the whole object graph is serialised. The hard part is not choosing fields, it
is the cross-references -- a unit's target is another unit, a building's garrison
points back at its occupants -- which is what the three registries below exist to
rebuild.
48582b70fd45:
Opened from the title screen the panel is read-only: loading, exporting,
importing and deleting only. The game behind the title screen has not started, so
saving it is meaningless -- and an accidental tap on save would overwrite a real
save irrecoverably.
2f1ebaffbd90:
The map-level tables worth saving are those that play changes and that are not
purely derived.
Vision, danger and last-seen are updated on a throttle, a few seconds apart, and
must not be recomputed on load: recomputing would advance tiles that were not due
yet, leaving the world one throttle phase away from where it was saved. That
breaks determinism, and the fingerprint test catches it.
80fea07e93be:
Byte-level PackBits RLE plus base64. Terrain, fog and forest tables are mostly
long runs of one value, so they compress to a few percent -- uncompressed they
would add roughly 150KB per save.
3743cb8f5183:
Registry 1: static tables, keyed by path string. Within one build, loading is
deterministic, so both save and load compute the same set of paths.
6e5769b42b17:
Registry 3: containers, computed on both sides at run time, so a token resolves
to the live object and can be refilled in place -- which is what keeps the module-
level aliases pointing at something real.
b34edea9015e:
Slot management. A small metadata record is stored under its own key so listing
the slots does not have to parse a whole save.
98a7bafde537:
Loading into a different map records the pending save and reloads at the right
map URL: the map is fixed at load time and the world's dimensions derive from it,
so a reload is the only correct route.
463d837647f9: Save/load panel, reachable from the tools menu and from the title screen.
